name: JST XH
useCases: LED strips, small batteries, Arduino shields, hobby electronics
notes: Top-entry latch on header; do not confuse with VH (3.96mm pitch). Very common in consumer electronics.

name: JST VH
useCases: UPS batteries, power supplies, CCTV power, larger loads than XH
notes: Larger pitch sibling of XH; used where higher current needed on PCB.

name: JST PH
useCases: LiPo battery packs, drone batteries, IoT devices, GPS modules
notes: De facto standard for small LiPo balance leads and compact electronics.

name: JST SH
useCases: Qwiic/STEMMA QT, compact sensors, IMU breakouts, camera modules
notes: 1mm pitch; confused with GH visually. SH is shorter mating height than GH.

name: JST GH
useCases: Pixhawk autopilot, drone FC peripherals, GPS, telemetry, airspeed sensors
notes: Pixhawk standard. Looks like SH but 1.25mm pitch and has a more prominent latch. Not interchangeable with SH.

name: JST ZH
useCases: Ultra-compact wearables, micro drones, tiny sensors
notes: Smallest common JST series; fragile, not for repeated mating.

name: JST SM
useCases: LED strip inline connections, fan connections, small appliance internal wiring
notes: Wire-to-wire variant; common in LED lighting and consumer appliance harnesses.

name: JST RCY
useCases: RC battery packs, small DC motors, toys, battery holders
notes: 2-pin only; ubiquitous in RC hobby and toy battery connections.

name: Molex KK 254
useCases: PC internal power (legacy), hobbyist boards, 3D printer boards
notes: Classic 0.1" pitch; often called generic 'Molex connector' in hobbyist context.

name: Molex Micro-Fit 3.0
useCases: ATX power (CPU 4/8-pin, PCIe 6/8-pin), server power, industrial power distribution on PCB
notes: Dual-row; gold or tin contacts. Basis of ATX12V CPU and GPU power connectors.

name: Molex Mini-Fit Jr.
useCases: ATX main 24-pin, GPU power, server backplanes, EV auxiliary power
notes: The ATX 24-pin motherboard connector is this family. Very robust for board-level power.

name: Molex Nano-Fit
useCases: Space-constrained power delivery, compact robotics, medical devices
notes: Smaller profile than Micro-Fit; good for dense PCB layouts.

name: XT30
useCases: Small drones (whoop class), LiPo 2–3S, ESC up to 30A
notes: Gold-plated bullet contacts. Nylon housing. Polarized to prevent reverse connection.

name: XT60
useCases: FPV drones, RC cars, 3–6S LiPo, portable power stations, e-bikes
notes: Industry standard for mid-power RC/drone. XT60H variant has housing cap for unused side.

name: XT90
useCases: Large RC vehicles, e-bike batteries, high-current LiPo 6S+, BMS output
notes: Anti-spark variant (XT90-S) has built-in resistor for pre-charge. Used in DIY EV and BESS.

name: Anderson Powerpole (15/30/45)
useCases: Ham radio, solar charge controllers, portable power, emergency comms, robotics
notes: Genderless; color-coded by circuit. ARES/RACES standard for emergency comms. Stackable to any pin count.

name: Anderson SB50
useCases: Forklift batteries, golf carts, UPS battery strings, industrial chargers
notes: Color-coded by voltage (red=24V, gray=36V, blue=48V). Heavy-duty industrial standard.

name: Anderson SB120 / SB175 / SB350
useCases: Large forklift/AGV batteries, solar battery banks, EV auxiliary, BESS modules
notes: Same keyed-color system as SB50. SB350 used in large BESS interconnects and EV traction battery service disconnects.

name: DC Barrel Jack (5.5x2.1mm)
useCases: Routers, CCTV cameras, LED drivers, Arduino, Raspberry Pi, lab equipment
notes: Most common size is 5.5x2.1mm. Center-positive is convention but NOT universal — verify polarity. EIAJ standard sizes exist but many are non-standard.

name: DC Barrel Jack (5.5x2.5mm)
useCases: Laptops (older), monitors, higher-power peripherals
notes: Looks identical to 2.1mm but won't seat properly — common source of intermittent power failures.

name: 2.1mm Locking DC Barrel (Switchcraft/Kycon)
useCases: Professional audio gear, rack equipment, industrial sensors, outdoor cameras
notes: Prevents accidental disconnection. Switchcraft S760/S761 series common in pro audio.

name: Tamiya / Mini-Tamiya
useCases: RC cars, airsoft batteries, older RC chargers
notes: Being replaced by XT series in RC hobby. High contact resistance at higher currents. Mini-Tamiya common in airsoft.

name: Deans Ultra (T-Plug)
useCases: RC aircraft, older FPV drones, RC chargers
notes: Largely replaced by XT60 in drone/RC. Genuine Deans vs clones differ in contact quality.

name: EC3 / EC5
useCases: Horizon Hobby / E-flite RC aircraft and batteries, some e-bikes
notes: Bullet-style like XT but different housing. EC5 used in high-current e-bike and large RC applications.

name: Wurth/Phoenix Pluggable PCB Terminal Block
useCases: Industrial PLCs, motor drivers, CNC controllers, servo amplifier power input
notes: Not just terminal blocks — pluggable variants allow field wiring without soldering. Green/orange Phoenix, blue Wago standard colors.

name: Wago 221 Lever Nut
useCases: Domestic junction boxes, LED driver wiring, panel wiring, DIN rail power distribution
notes: Transparent body for visual wire verification. Replacing traditional wire nuts globally. Not suitable for stranded wire without ferrules.

name: Wago 2060/2061 PCB SMD
useCases: LED modules, compact IoT boards, lighting PCBs
notes: SMD-mountable push-in connector. Designed for automated PCB assembly in lighting industry.

name: Ferrule / Bootlace Ferrule
useCases: Crimped onto stranded wire before inserting into screw/spring terminals, PLC wiring, panel boards
notes: Not a connector per se but mandatory wire termination for European/Indian panel wiring. Color-coded by wire gauge (DIN standard).

name: ATX 24-pin Main Power
useCases: Desktop PC motherboard main power
notes: Based on Molex Mini-Fit Jr. 20-pin legacy + 4-pin auxiliary. Keyed to prevent mis-insertion.

name: ATX EPS 4/8-pin CPU Power
useCases: Desktop PC CPU power, server motherboard power
notes: Do not confuse 8-pin CPU (4+4) with 8-pin PCIe (6+2). Different keying.

name: PCIe 6-pin / 8-pin / 12+4 (12VHPWR)
useCases: GPU power, high-end graphics cards
notes: 12VHPWR (ATX 3.0) replaces multiple 8-pin. Had early melting issues — ensure proper seating. 12V-2x6 is refined successor.

name: SATA Power
useCases: HDD, SSD, optical drives, SATA accessories
notes: 3.3V rail rarely used. L-key prevents reverse insertion. Molex-to-SATA adapters are fire hazards (molded type).

name: Molex 4-pin Peripheral (Legacy)
useCases: Legacy IDE HDDs, case fans, old peripherals
notes: Being phased out. Still found in fan adapters and older power supplies. Tight fit causes connector damage.

name: Floppy 4-pin (Berg)
useCases: Floppy drives, some front-panel USB, legacy card readers
notes: Nearly extinct outside retro computing.

name: Banana Plug / Socket (4mm)
useCases: Lab power supplies, multimeters, test equipment, educational labs
notes: Shrouded (safety) banana mandatory in EU for lab equipment. 4mm is universal; 2mm exists for lower current.

name: PP30 / PP75 / PP120 (Powerpole)
useCases: Solar combiner boxes, battery banks, ham radio high-power, EV auxiliary
notes: Larger siblings of PP15/30/45. Used in serious solar and BESS installations.

name: Faston / Quick-Connect (6.3mm / 4.8mm / 2.8mm)
useCases: Appliance internal wiring, relay connections, automotive fuse boxes, transformer taps, Indian MCB wiring
notes: 6.3mm most common in appliances. Insulated variants color-coded by wire gauge (red=22-18, blue=16-14, yellow=12-10 AWG).

name: IEC 60320 C13/C14
useCases: Desktop PCs, monitors, servers, lab instruments, UPS, network switches
notes: Most ubiquitous AC power connector globally. C13=female cable end, C14=male inlet on device.

name: IEC 60320 C15/C16 (Notched, Hot)
useCases: Electric kettles, rice cookers, high-temp appliances, some UPS
notes: Higher temp rating than C13/C14 (120°C vs 70°C). Notch prevents using standard C13 cable on hot appliance inlet.

name: IEC 60320 C19/C20
useCases: Server racks, PDUs, large UPS, data center power, high-power networking
notes: Larger rectangular form factor for higher current. C19=female cable, C20=male device inlet.

name: IEC 60320 C5/C6 (Cloverleaf/Mickey)
useCases: Laptop power bricks, projectors, portable instruments, some printers
notes: Three-round-pin cloverleaf shape. Nearly universal for laptop AC adapters.

name: IEC 60320 C7/C8 (Figure-8)
useCases: Phone chargers, small appliances, PlayStation, portable radios, laptop bricks (lower power)
notes: No earth pin — for double-insulated devices only. Polarized variant (C7P) has one squared end.

name: IEC 60320 C1/C2 (Shaver)
useCases: Electric shavers, toothbrush chargers, very low power appliances
notes: Rarely seen in new designs; most shaver chargers now use USB-C or proprietary magnetic.

name: Indian IS 1293 Type D (5A/6A)
useCases: Indian domestic wiring — lights, fans, phone chargers, small appliances
notes: Standard 3-pin round plug unique to India/South Asia. 5A socket won't accept 16A plug — pin diameter differs.

name: Indian IS 1293 Type M (16A)
useCases: Indian domestic wiring — ACs, geysers, washing machines, ovens, high-power appliances
notes: Larger pin diameter than 5A variant. Indian standard mandates separate 16A circuit with higher gauge wiring.

name: Indian Universal Socket
useCases: Indian domestic — accepts IS 1293 5A, Type C (Euro), and sometimes Type G (UK) plugs
notes: Not technically compliant with strict IEC standards due to exposed pin risk. Being phased out in favor of dedicated sockets with shutters.

name: NEMA 5-15P/R
useCases: North American standard wall outlet — all general-purpose appliances, electronics
notes: Most common power plug in North America and Japan (Japan uses ungrounded 2-pin variant).

name: NEMA 5-20P/R
useCases: North American 20A circuits — kitchen appliances, workshop tools, server racks
notes: T-shaped neutral slot accepts both 5-15 and 5-20 plugs. 5-15 plug fits in 5-20 receptacle but not vice versa.

name: NEMA L14-30P/R (Twist-Lock)
useCases: Generator transfer switches, portable generator output, RV power, workshop 240V
notes: Locking prevents accidental disconnection. Common on portable generators and manual transfer switches.

name: NEMA 14-50P/R
useCases: EV home charging (Level 2), electric ranges, welders, RV parks
notes: De facto standard for Level 2 home EV charging in North America.

name: Schuko (CEE 7/4) / Type F
useCases: European domestic — Germany, France, India (some), most of continental Europe
notes: Symmetric — no polarity enforcement. Earth connection via side clips, not a pin.

name: Type G (BS 1363)
useCases: UK, Singapore, Hong Kong, UAE, some Indian hotels
notes: Fused plug (3A or 13A cartridge fuse inside plug). Shuttered socket is very safe design.

name: Type C (Europlug, CEE 7/16)
useCases: European low-power — phone chargers, lamps, shavers
notes: No earth — for double-insulated Class II devices only. Fits into Schuko, Type E, Type J, Type L sockets.

name: IEC 60309 (CEE Industrial)
useCases: Factory power feeds, construction sites, stage/event power, data center temp power
notes: Color-coded by voltage: Yellow=110V, Blue=230V, Red=400V, Black=500V+. Pin position clock-keying prevents cross-voltage mating. Mandatory on construction sites in EU/India.

name: Stage Pin (20A)
useCases: Theater/stage lighting, entertainment industry (North America)
notes: Common in US theater installations. Being partially replaced by powerCON in new venues.

name: Clipsal / HPM (Australian)
useCases: Australian/NZ domestic power
notes: Similar to Chinese GB 2099 but not identical. Angled flat pins in inverted-V pattern.

name: Cam-Lok (E1016)
useCases: Temporary power distribution, generator tie-ins, stage/event power, mining, shipyard
notes: Color-coded single-pole: Black/Red/Blue=phase, White=neutral, Green=ground. Industry standard for entertainment and construction temp power.

name: Powerlock
useCases: Concert/event power, large generator distribution, offshore rigs, shipyard shore power
notes: Successor to Cam-Lok for higher current. Finger-safe design. IP67 when mated. Used for 400kW+ event power.

name: Busbar Connector / Joint
useCases: Switchgear, MCC (motor control centers), busway/bus duct, panel boards
notes: Not a plug-in connector per se — bolted busbar joints with Belleville washers and specified torque. Some MCC have plug-in stab connectors.

name: MCC Stab / Plug-in Busbar
useCases: Motor control center plug-in units, draw-out circuit breakers
notes: Allows hot-swap of MCC buckets/drawers. Stab contacts engage when unit is racked in.

name: Dinse (Welding Connector)
useCases: Welding machines (MIG/TIG/Stick), plasma cutters, battery pack testing, electroplating
notes: 10-25mm, 35-50mm, 50-70mm sizes correspond to cable cross-section. Bayonet quarter-turn lock.

name: J1772 (Type 1 / SAE J1772)
useCases: North American / Japanese EVs — Nissan Leaf, older GM, Toyota
notes: Being superseded by NACS/Tesla connector in North America. Still standard in Japan.

name: Type 2 (Mennekes / IEC 62196-2)
useCases: European EVs — all EU vehicles, Indian EVs (Tata, Mahindra), universal AC charging
pins: 7 (L1, L2, L3, N, Earth, CP, PP)
notes: EU-mandated standard. Single-phase (32A/7.4kW) or three-phase (63A/43kW). Indian EV AC charging also adopting Type 2.

name: CCS1 (Combo 1)
useCases: North American DC fast charging — all non-Tesla US EVs pre-NACS
notes: J1772 AC section on top + 2 large DC pins below. Being phased out in US as NACS becomes standard.

name: CCS2 (Combo 2)
useCases: European DC fast charging — all EU EVs, Indian DC chargers
notes: EU-mandated DC fast charging standard. Also adopted by India for DC fast charging (Bharat DC 001).

name: CHAdeMO
useCases: Japanese EVs (Nissan, Mitsubishi), some older EU installations
notes: Supports V2G (vehicle-to-grid) bidirectional charging. ChaoJi (CHAdeMO 3.0) is joint China-Japan effort for 900kW.

name: GB/T 20234 (China)
useCases: Chinese domestic EVs — BYD, NIO, XPeng, all China-market vehicles
notes: Mandatory in China. Not compatible with CCS or CHAdeMO. ChaoJi may unify with CHAdeMO 3.0.

name: Tesla NACS (SAE J3400)
useCases: Tesla vehicles, all new North American EVs from 2025+
current: AC: 48A / DC: 350A (up to 350kW on V4 Supercharger)
notes: Compact design — AC and DC on same pins. Adopted as North American standard. Tesla opened Supercharger network via Magic Dock/NACS adapters.

name: Bharat AC-001
useCases: Indian domestic slow AC charging — Tata Nexon, Mahindra XUV400 (basic chargers)
notes: India-specific low-cost AC charging standard. Based on modified industrial plug for cost. Type 2 IEC preferred for higher-power AC.

name: Bharat DC-001
useCases: Indian public DC fast charging — government FAME scheme chargers
notes: Uses CCS2 physical connector but with India-specific protocol layer. Low-cost 15kW variant deployed widely under government FAME-II scheme.

name: MC4 (Multi-Contact 4mm)
useCases: Solar panel string connections, combiner boxes, string inverter inputs
notes: Industry standard for solar PV. Genuine Stäubli MC4 vs compatible clones — cross-brand mating NOT recommended (UL violation). Requires MC4 crimping tool.

name: MC4-Evo 2 (Stäubli)
useCases: 1500V solar systems, utility-scale PV, bifacial panel strings
notes: Next-gen MC4 for 1500V systems. Not backward compatible with MC4 without adapter.

name: Amphenol Helios H4 / UTX
useCases: Solar panel connections (MC4 competitor), combiner boxes, string inverters
notes: Amphenol's MC4-compatible family. H4 is cross-matable with genuine MC4; UTX is proprietary.

name: Phoenix Contact Sunclix
useCases: Solar inverter DC input (SMA, Fronius), combiner boxes
notes: Tool-free insertion and release. Used as standard DC input on many SMA inverters.

name: Tyco/TE Solarlok
useCases: Solar panels, junction boxes, microinverter connections
notes: Being replaced by MC4 variants. Not cross-matable with MC4.

name: Battery Terminal Clamp (Top-Post)
useCases: Automotive lead-acid batteries, golf carts, marine batteries
notes: Tapered conical post. Positive post is larger diameter than negative. Corrosion at terminal is common failure mode.

name: Battery Terminal Lug (Crimp/Bolt)
useCases: BESS racks (LFP cells), telecom -48V battery strings, UPS battery cabinets, forklift batteries
notes: Copper or copper-tinned lugs crimped to battery cable. Torque specification critical — under-torque causes arcing.

name: Rema Connector (DIN 160A / 320A)
useCases: European forklift batteries, pallet jacks, BESS module interconnects, industrial AGVs
notes: Standard European forklift battery connector. Color-coded by voltage. DIN 43589 spec.

name: Generator Receptacle (L14-30 / L21-30 / CS6375)
useCases: Portable generators, standby generators, ATS connections, RV parks
notes: L14-30 most common on portable generators. CS6375 (California Standard) used on RV generators. Always use with transfer switch — never backfeed main panel.

name: Shore Power (Marinco / SmartPlug)
useCases: Marine shore power (boats/yachts), houseboat hookups
notes: Marinco twist-lock standard for 30+ years. SmartPlug rectangular design resists overheating at connection point.

name: Pantograph Contact Strip
useCases: Electric train overhead catenary current collection — mainline, metro, high-speed rail
notes: Carbon or copper-graphite strip on spring-loaded pantograph arm. Indian Railways uses 25kV AC 50Hz single-phase overhead catenary.

name: Third Rail Shoe / Collector Shoe
useCases: Metro/subway third rail systems — Delhi Metro, London Underground, NYC Subway
notes: Top-contact, bottom-contact, or side-contact depending on system. Delhi Metro uses standard-gauge side-contact 750V DC third rail.

name: Jumper Cable / Inter-Coach Connector (ICF/LHB)
useCases: Indian Railways inter-coach power and control connections, ICF and LHB coaches
notes: Old ICF coaches used 110V DC from under-slung alternator. LHB coaches use 750V AC HOG (Head-on Generation) from locomotive. RDSO is Railway Standards Organization.

name: WAP/WAG Locomotive Terminal Block
useCases: Indian electric locomotive internal power distribution — WAP-7, WAG-9, WAG-12B
notes: Internal busbars and terminal blocks rated for traction voltages. Bolted connections with specified torque values. WAG-12B (12000HP) is modern Alstom Prima locomotive.

name: Train Communication Network (TCN) Connector
useCases: Modern train communication buses — Kavach TCAS, ETCS, LHB coach diagnostics
notes: Wire Train Bus (WTB) for inter-vehicle, Multifunction Vehicle Bus (MVB) for intra-vehicle. Kavach (Indian TCAS) uses standard TCN physical layer.

name: Coupler Head Electrical Connector (AAR)
useCases: US/Indian freight locomotive MU (Multiple Unit) connections, passenger coach control jumpers
notes: Rides on mechanical coupler. Contacts engage when couplers fully engaged. Used for MU locomotive operation.

name: MV Cable Termination / Elbow Connector (200A Loadbreak)
useCases: 11kV / 33kV underground cable terminations, RMU (Ring Main Unit) connections, pad-mount transformers
notes: Screened separable connector. 200A loadbreak can be operated under load. 600A deadbreak requires de-energization. Standard for underground MV distribution in India and globally.

name: HV Bushing (Transformer/Switchgear)
useCases: Power transformer HV terminals, GIS (Gas Insulated Switchgear) connections, 132kV/220kV/400kV substations
notes: Porcelain or polymer-housed. Oil-to-air or oil-to-SF6 interface. BIL (Basic Insulation Level) rated. Indian grid uses 132/220/400/765kV classes.

name: GIS Plug-in Connector
useCases: Gas Insulated Switchgear internal connections, cable-to-GIS interface
locking: Bolted flange with O-ring SF6 seal
notes: Plug-in design allows modular GIS bay extension. Tulip-type or finger-type contacts. SF6 gas insulated.

name: Overhead Line T-Connector / Piercing Connector
useCases: Overhead distribution taps, service drops, Indian LT line connections, streetlight feeds
notes: Insulation-piercing connectors (IPC) don't require cable stripping. Standard for Indian electricity board LT/HT overhead tapping.

name: CT/PT Secondary Terminal Block
useCases: Current transformer and potential transformer secondary connections in substations and metering panels
notes: CT secondary terminals MUST have shorting links — open CT secondary generates lethal voltage. Standard in Indian substation protection panels.

name: XLPE Cable Lug / Compression Connector
useCases: Underground cable terminations, transformer connections, panel bus connections, Indian HT cable work
notes: Copper/aluminium bimetallic lugs. Must be hydraulically crimped with calibrated dies. Indian standard brands: Dowells, Comet, Polycab. Cable gland + heat-shrink termination kit needed for MV/HV.

name: Link Disconnector / Drop-out Fuse
useCases: 11kV/33kV overhead line protection, transformer fuse protection, Indian rural distribution
notes: Fuse element drops when blown, providing visible break. Hot-line tools used for operation. Standard on every Indian 11kV transformer pole.

name: DE-9 (DB-9) Male/Female
useCases: RS-232 serial ports, CNC controllers (Fanuc, Haas), PLC programming ports, industrial HMI, GPS receivers, older lab instruments
notes: Often called DB-9 incorrectly — correct nomenclature is DE-9 (E-size shell). RS-232 on DE-9 is alive in CNC, PLC, and industrial automation. Pinout differs between DTE and DCE.

name: DA-15 (DB-15)
useCases: Game port (legacy), older AUI Ethernet, some industrial I/O, MIDI interfaces (rare)
notes: Two-row variant. Game port is extinct. AUI Ethernet extinct. Some legacy industrial I/O cards still use it.

name: DB-15HD (DE-15 / VGA)
useCases: VGA monitors, projectors (legacy), KVM switches, some industrial HMI panels
notes: Three-row high-density variant. VGA officially deprecated by Intel/AMD in 2015 but still on millions of projectors, KVM switches, and industrial HMIs.

name: DB-25 Male/Female
useCases: RS-232 full-pinout (original IBM PC), parallel printer port (IEEE 1284), SCSI (original), industrial serial
notes: Original RS-232 connector before DE-9 became common. 25-pin parallel printer port replaced by Centronics then USB.

name: DC-37
useCases: Industrial data acquisition, multi-axis motion controllers, older PLC I/O modules, military comm
notes: Still used in high-channel-count DAQ and industrial motion control (Galil, Parker).

name: DD-50
useCases: SCSI-2 connections, high-density industrial I/O, some telecom patch panels
notes: High-density 50-pin D-sub. Replaced by SCA-2 and then SAS in SCSI applications.

name: D-sub Combo (Power+Signal)
useCases: Industrial servo drives, CNC stepper drivers, robotics motor controllers, medical equipment
notes: Combines standard D-sub signal pins with larger power contacts and/or coax contacts in same shell. Common on servo amplifiers (Copley, AMC).

name: Centronics 36-pin (IEEE 1284-B)
useCases: Parallel printers (legacy), CNC file transfer (legacy), some industrial controllers
notes: The classic printer connector. Micro-Centronics 36 (HPCN36) was the compact variant on SCSI devices. Completely replaced by USB.

name: DIN-5 (180° / 240°)
useCases: AT keyboard (legacy), MIDI (DIN-5 180°), old European audio equipment, some amateur radio
notes: MIDI DIN-5 is the 180° pin arrangement and is still standard for hardware synthesizers and music production, even as TRS MIDI gains ground. AT keyboard DIN-5 replaced by PS/2.

name: Mini-DIN 6 (PS/2)
useCases: PS/2 keyboard and mouse ports on legacy PCs, some KVM switches, industrial panel PCs
legacy: Yes — Legacy (still on some industrial PCs)
notes: Purple = keyboard, Green = mouse. Hot-plug not officially supported (but usually works). Some BIOS/UEFI still requires PS/2 for keyboard access where USB isn't initialized.

name: Mini-DIN 4 (S-Video)
useCases: S-Video output on DVD players, video capture cards, surveillance DVRs, projectors
notes: Separates luminance (Y) and chrominance (C) for better quality than composite. Dead in consumer; still on some legacy surveillance DVRs.

name: Mini-DIN 8
useCases: Apple serial (legacy Mac), LocalTalk networking, some label printers
notes: Apple's serial port before USB. LocalTalk was Apple's pre-Ethernet networking.

name: RJ11 / RJ12 (6P2C / 6P4C / 6P6C)
useCases: PSTN telephone lines, DSL modems, fax machines, PBX systems, RS-232 console on some Cisco gear
notes: RJ11 is 6P2C (2 conductors), RJ12 is 6P6C (all 6). Commonly confused. Still used for DSL, alarm panels, and some industrial RS-485 serial.

name: RJ45 (8P8C) — for serial/console
useCases: Cisco/Juniper console port (RS-232 over RJ45), Ethernet (covered in Phase 3), some RS-485 serial
notes: Cisco blue console cable: RJ45 to DB-9. Pinout is NOT Ethernet T568A/B — it's a rollover cable. Newer gear uses USB-C or USB-B mini for console.

name: RS-485 / RS-422 on Screw Terminal
useCases: MODBUS RTU, BMS (building management), industrial sensors, VFD communication, DMX512 lighting
notes: Many RS-485 devices use bare screw terminals rather than a specific connector. Wire polarity (A/B) convention is inconsistent across vendors — always verify with scope.

name: 4-20mA Current Loop Terminal
useCases: Industrial sensors (pressure, flow, level, temp), PLCs analog input, DCS systems, SCADA
notes: Not a connector type but a signaling standard that uses generic screw terminals. 4mA=zero, 20mA=full scale. Wire break = 0mA, detectable as fault. Still the dominant industrial analog interface.

name: 0-10V Analog Signal Terminal
useCases: HVAC damper actuators, lighting dimmers (0-10V), VFD speed reference, simple sensor outputs
notes: Simpler than 4-20mA but susceptible to voltage drop over distance and noise. Common in building automation HVAC.

name: MIDI TRS (3.5mm Type A/B)
useCases: Compact MIDI on synthesizers, Korg, Arturia, some Novation controllers
notes: Type A (endorsed by MIDI Assn) and Type B (Korg, older) are NOT compatible — pin swap between ring and tip. Replacing DIN-5 MIDI on space-constrained gear.

name: IEEE 488 / GPIB (Centronics-like 24-pin)
useCases: Lab instruments (oscilloscopes, signal generators, spectrum analyzers), automated test equipment (ATE), semiconductor test
maxLength: 20m total bus (2m per device, 15 devices max)
notes: Created by HP (hence HP-IB). Stackable connectors allow daisy-chain. 1MB/s (IEEE 488.1), 8MB/s (HS488). Being replaced by LXI (Ethernet-based) but still standard in semiconductor ATE and calibration labs.

name: SCPI/VISA over USB-TMC
useCases: Modern lab instruments replacing GPIB — Keysight, R&S, Tektronix oscilloscopes and signal generators
notes: Uses standard USB-B connector but with USBTMC device class. Same SCPI command set as GPIB. Requires VISA driver (NI-VISA or Keysight IO Libraries).

name: 1-Wire (Dallas/Maxim)
useCases: DS18B20 temperature sensors, iButton access control, battery fuel gauges (DS2438), EEPROM
notes: Not a connector standard but a protocol that uses various physical connectors. iButton uses stainless steel can with F5 contact reader. DS18B20 uses generic headers or screw terminals.

name: I²C on JST/Qwiic/STEMMA QT
useCases: SparkFun Qwiic, Adafruit STEMMA QT, sensor breakouts, IMUs, OLED displays, ADCs
notes: Standardized I²C connector on JST SH 1mm. Qwiic and STEMMA QT are cross-compatible. Daisy-chainable. 3.3V logic only (5V will damage).

name: M12 A-coded (4/5-pin)
useCases: Proximity sensors, photoelectric sensors, PLC digital I/O, industrial Ethernet (4-pin only up to 100Mbps), PROFINET, EtherNet/IP
notes: Most common industrial sensor connector worldwide. A-coded 4-pin also used for 100Mbps industrial Ethernet. 5-pin for sensors needing two outputs or analog. IP67 when mated.

name: M12 B-coded
useCases: PROFIBUS DP, some DeviceNet, older fieldbus applications
notes: PROFIBUS-specific coding. Being replaced by Ethernet-based protocols (PROFINET on A/D-coded). Still widespread in legacy chemical/process plants.

name: M12 C-coded
useCases: AC-powered sensors, some AC solenoid valves, older European AC sensor standard
notes: AC-rated variant for valve manifolds and AC sensors. Being replaced by IO-Link on A-coded.

name: M12 D-coded (4-pin)
useCases: PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, industrial Ethernet up to 100Mbps, machine vision cameras (GigE partial)
notes: Standard for 100Mbps industrial Ethernet. D-coding prevents mating with A-coded. IP67. For Gigabit use X-coded.

name: M12 X-coded (8-pin)
useCases: Gigabit industrial Ethernet, PROFINET over GbE, high-bandwidth machine vision, industrial IoT gateways
notes: 4 twisted pairs in M12 shell for 1Gbps/10Gbps. Larger diameter than A/D-coded M12. Required for GigE Vision cameras in harsh environments.

name: M12 L-coded
useCases: 24V DC power for industrial switches, IO-Link masters, field power distribution boxes
notes: Power variant of M12 — larger pins for higher current. Used to power field devices that previously needed hardwired terminal blocks.

name: M12 K-coded
useCases: AC power for industrial devices, 230V/400V field devices
notes: AC power variant of M12. Less common than L-coded DC power.

name: M12 S-coded
useCases: High-current DC power distribution in the field, DC motor drives, actuator power
notes: Fewer pins, higher current per pin than L-coded.

name: M12 T-coded
useCases: Even higher current DC field power, same concept as S-coded but different pin geometry
notes: Alternative high-power M12 coding. Not widely adopted outside specific vendor ecosystems.

name: M8 (3/4-pin)
useCases: Compact proximity sensors, miniature photoelectric sensors, limited-space installations, collaborative robots
notes: Smaller than M12 — used where space is critical. IP67. Common on compact sensors from IFM, Sick, Balluff, Omron.

name: M5 (3/4-pin)
useCases: Ultra-compact sensors, micro proximity switches, precision measurement probes
notes: Smallest standard industrial circular connector. Limited to very small sensors in tight spaces.

name: 7/8" (M7/8) Circular
useCases: DeviceNet trunk cables, AS-i power, industrial robot power feeds, servo motor power
notes: Larger than M12 for higher power. DeviceNet trunk historically used 7/8" but migrating to Ethernet. Still used for AC/DC motor power feeds in the field.

name: Phoenix COMBICON (MC/MCV/MSTB series)
useCases: PLC I/O modules, industrial controller PCBs, VFD control boards, power supply output terminals, servo drive signal terminals
notes: Pluggable PCB terminal blocks. MC=fixed header, MCV=vertical, MSTB=horizontal. Green is Phoenix standard color. Equivalent ranges from Weidmüller (BL/SL), Wago (2000-series), Würth, Dinkle.

name: Weidmüller BL/SL PCB Terminal
useCases: PLC modules, panel meter boards, industrial PSU terminals, sensor interface PCBs
notes: Direct competitor to Phoenix COMBICON. Interchangeable at same pitch within screw-type families. Orange is Weidmüller standard color.

name: DIN Rail Terminal Block (Phoenix UK/UT, Weidmüller WDU, Wago TOPJOB)
useCases: Panel wiring, motor starter circuits, control panel I/O marshalling, PLC power distribution, Indian electrical panels
notes: Snaps onto 35mm DIN rail (EN 60715). Bridgeable with jumper combs. End stops, partition plates, marking tags available. Phoenix UK series is most common globally including India.

name: DIN Rail Fuse Terminal Block
useCases: Instrument circuit protection, sensor loop fusing, signal line fusing in marshalling cabinets
notes: Holds 5x20mm or 6.3x32mm glass/ceramic fuses. LED blown-fuse indication available.

name: DIN Rail Disconnect Terminal Block
useCases: CT secondary marshalling, relay test blocks, protection circuit isolation in substations and MCC
notes: Knife-disconnect allows isolating circuits without removing wires. Essential in protection relay panels for CT secondary circuits. Shorting versions available for CTs.

name: DeviceNet Connector (Micro/Mini)
useCases: Allen-Bradley DeviceNet networks, packaging machines, automotive assembly, material handling
notes: DeviceNet uses CAN-based protocol. Micro-style uses M12, Mini-style uses 7/8" connectors. Being replaced by EtherNet/IP on M12 D-coded.

name: AS-Interface (AS-i) Cable/Connector
useCases: Simple sensor/actuator networks, conveyor control, valve islands, pick-to-light systems
notes: Yellow flat cable with insulation-piercing taps — no stripping needed. Power and data on same 2-wire cable. Being replaced by IO-Link.

name: IO-Link (on M12 A-coded)
useCases: Smart sensors (IO-Link enabled), parameterizable sensors, condition monitoring, predictive maintenance
notes: Uses standard M12 A-coded 3-wire sensor cable. IO-Link master on PLC side. Point-to-point, not a bus. Up to 230.4 kbps. Backward compatible with standard digital sensors on same cable.

name: PROFIBUS PA Connector
useCases: Process instruments (pressure, flow, level, temp), hazardous area devices, DCS field instruments
locking: Screw terminal (on device), M12 B-coded or 7/8"
notes: Intrinsically safe variant of PROFIBUS for process industry. Manchester-encoded, 31.25kbps. Being replaced by Ethernet-APL (2-wire Ethernet for process).

name: HART (on 4-20mA terminals)
useCases: Process instruments — superimposes digital data on 4-20mA current loop, Emerson/Honeywell/Yokogawa transmitters
notes: Coexists with 4-20mA on same wiring. Frequency-shift keying at 1200Hz/2200Hz doesn't disturb analog signal. WirelessHART uses 2.4GHz mesh. Most installed process instruments globally support HART.

name: Ethernet-APL (on 2-wire fieldbus)
useCases: Next-gen process instrumentation replacing PROFIBUS PA/Foundation Fieldbus, hazardous area Ethernet, Zone 0/1/2
voltage: Up to 60V DC (power over data line)
notes: 10Mbps Ethernet on 2-wire cable with power. Designed to replace all legacy fieldbuses in process industry. PROFINET over APL, EtherNet/IP over APL, HART-IP over APL all supported.

name: OBD-II (SAE J1962)
useCases: Vehicle diagnostics port — mandatory on all cars since 1996 (US) / 2001 (EU) / 2010 (India BS-IV)
notes: Protocols: CAN (pins 6+14), K-Line (pin 7), J1850 (pins 2+10), ISO 9141 (pin 7). Location: within 0.6m of steering column by regulation. Indian BS-VI mandates CAN-based OBD.

name: Deutsch DT (2/3/4/6/8/12-pin)
useCases: Heavy truck wiring, off-road equipment (CAT, John Deere), marine engines, racing harnesses, military vehicles, aftermarket ECU wiring
notes: Wedge-lock retains contacts. Silicone environmental seal provides IP69K. Size 16 (13A) and size 20 (7.5A) contacts. Requires DT crimp tool (HDT-48-00). Clone market is large — genuine TE/Deutsch matters for mil/aerospace.

name: Deutsch DTM (2–12-pin)
useCases: ECU signal connections, compact sensor harnesses, motorsport data logging, ABS/ESP sensor pigtails
notes: Smaller than DT — uses size 20 contacts only. More pin-dense. Same environmental sealing as DT.

name: Deutsch DTP (2/4-pin)
useCases: Relay connections, fuel pump power, high-current actuator feeds, auxiliary lighting power
notes: Uses larger size 12 contacts for high-current applications. Limited pin counts.

name: Deutsch HD (24–47-pin)
useCases: Heavy equipment ECUs (Cummins, CAT), transmission controllers, body controllers on trucks/buses
notes: High-density rectangular sealed connector. Used for main ECU harness connectors on heavy equipment.

name: AMP Superseal 1.5 (2–6-pin)
useCases: Automotive sensor connections, lighting harnesses, European trucks, body control modules
notes: Very common in European automotive OEM harnesses (BMW, VW, Mercedes). Silicone multi-wire seal.

name: TE/AMP MCP (2.8mm / 6.3mm / 9.5mm)
useCases: Relay sockets, fuse blocks, switch connections, headlight connectors, Indian two-wheeler wiring
notes: MCP = Multi-Contact Point. Similar concept to Faston but with better retention and sealed housing options. Ubiquitous in automotive relay sockets.

name: Molex MX150 (sealed)
useCases: GM/Chrysler OEM harnesses, lighting, body electronics, marine electronics, powersports
notes: Terminal Position Assurance (TPA) secondary lock. Silicone seal. GM-specified for many body wiring applications.

name: Delphi/Aptiv Metri-Pack 150/280/480/630
useCases: GM/Delphi OEM harnesses, engine bay connections, HVAC controls, body wiring, Indian Tata/Mahindra harnesses
notes: Number indicates terminal width in hundredths of inch (280 = 2.80mm). Sealed versions have silicone cavity plugs. Very common in Indian automotive OEM (Tata, Mahindra) via Delphi/Aptiv India.

name: Delphi/Aptiv Weather Pack
useCases: Aftermarket automotive wiring, marine electronics, outdoor power distribution, agricultural equipment
notes: Classic GM sealed connector. Silicone boot seals and cavity plugs. Tower-style square housings are distinctive. Common in aftermarket and marine.

name: Sumitomo HM/HW/MT Sealed
useCases: Japanese automotive OEM (Toyota, Honda, Nissan), motorcycle wiring (Yamaha, Kawasaki), Indian Suzuki/Honda
notes: HM (high moisture) and HW series common on Japanese domestic and export vehicles. Honda/Yamaha/Suzuki India also use Sumitomo variants.

name: Yazaki 090 / 060 Series
useCases: Toyota OEM harnesses, Lexus, some Mazda, ECU sub-harnesses, Japanese and Indian Toyota plants
notes: Number = terminal width in hundredths of mm (090=0.9mm). Toyota/Lexus standard. Available sealed and unsealed.

name: FAKRA (Automotive RF)
useCases: Automotive antenna connections (GPS, cellular, V2X, DAB/FM), infotainment head units, telematics modules, ADAS radar
notes: Color-coded by application: Blue=GPS, Black=cellular, Violet=GSM/UMTS, Wine=FM/AM, Cream=satellite radio, Green=V2X. Replacing SMB in automotive. HSD variant for high-speed data (LVDS camera links).

name: HSD (FAKRA High-Speed Data)
useCases: Automotive camera links (LVDS), display connections, Ethernet surround-view, ADAS camera feeds
notes: 4-contact version of FAKRA for broadband data. Carries LVDS video or Ethernet. Common for backup cameras and surround-view systems.

name: Mini50 / Nano MQS (TE)
useCases: ECU internal signal, gateway modules, instrument clusters, ADAS computing boxes, autonomous vehicle compute
notes: Ultra-fine-pitch for high-pin-count ECU connectors. Used inside sealed ECU enclosures for board-to-harness interface.

name: Automotive Blade Fuse Holders (ATO/ATC, Mini, Micro2)
useCases: Fuse boxes in all vehicles, aftermarket fuse taps, relay/fuse power distribution modules
notes: ATO/ATC (standard) most common globally. Mini and Micro2 in newer vehicles. Color-coded by amperage (Yellow=20A, Blue=15A, Red=10A, etc.).

name: Automotive Relay Socket (ISO Micro/Mini/Standard)
useCases: Horn relay, headlight relay, fuel pump relay, fan relay, starter relay — all vehicles
notes: ISO micro (280 series), ISO mini, and ISO standard (Bosch) relay footprints. DIN 72552 terminal numbering: 85/86=coil, 30=common, 87=NO, 87a=NC.

name: CAN Bus Termination (DB-9 / OBD / screw)
useCases: CAN 2.0A/B networks, J1939 heavy vehicle, CANopen industrial, NMEA 2000 marine, Indian BS-VI CAN diagnostics
locking: Varies (DB-9 per CiA, screw per OEM)
notes: CAN bus uses 120Ω termination at each end. CiA 303-1 standardizes DE-9 pinout for CANopen. J1939 uses Deutsch HD connectors. CAN-FD supports up to 8Mbps.

name: LIN Bus (on OEM connectors)
useCases: Window motors, mirror control, seat position, rain sensors, simple body functions
notes: Single-wire sub-bus for low-bandwidth body functions. Slave nodes are cheap. Master (ECU) bridges to CAN. Uses whatever connector the OEM chooses — no dedicated LIN connector standard.

name: SAE J1708 / J1587 (DE-9)
useCases: Legacy US heavy truck diagnostics, Cummins/CAT/Detroit engines, school buses, Indian Ashok Leyland (older)
notes: Pre-CAN heavy vehicle data bus. Still present on many in-service trucks and buses. Indian older Ashok Leyland and Tata trucks may have J1708 alongside newer CAN.

name: NMEA 2000 / DeviceNet Micro
useCases: Marine instruments (GPS, depth sounder, AIS, wind, fuel flow), boat networks, NMEA 2000 backbone
notes: CAN 2.0B at 250kbps. Proprietary connector (DeviceNet Micro-C) with T-connectors for drop lines. Powered bus — devices draw power from cable.

name: Hiperface DSL (on M12/M23 or direct)
useCases: Servo motor absolute encoders, single-cable servo drives (Bosch Rexroth, Lenze, B&R)
notes: Digital Servo Link — encoder data transmitted over motor power cable, eliminating separate feedback cable. Competitive with EnDat and BiSS.

name: EnDat 2.2 (on M12/M23)
useCases: Heidenhain linear encoders, servo motor feedback (Siemens SINAMICS), CNC machine axes, high-precision motion
notes: Bidirectional serial interface. Pure digital since EnDat 2.1. 25-bit+ absolute position. Used on Heidenhain linear and rotary encoders. Siemens drives natively support EnDat.

name: BiSS-C (on M12 or cable)
useCases: Open-standard absolute encoder interface, iC-Haus encoders, third-party servo encoders, Renishaw
notes: Open-source alternative to proprietary EnDat/Hiperface. Bidirectional serial, SSI-compatible. Adopted by Renishaw, Kübler, POSITAL.

name: SSI (Synchronous Serial Interface)
useCases: Absolute rotary encoders, linear encoders, industrial position measurement, CNC secondary feedback
notes: Unidirectional — master sends clock, slave returns position. Gray or binary code. Simple and reliable. Used on many industrial absolute encoders.

name: Incremental Encoder (A/B/Z on M12/M23 or cable)
useCases: Servo and stepper motor feedback, spindle speed sensing, conveyor speed, CNC spindle encoder
pins: A, B, Z (+ complements for differential = 6 signals) + supply
notes: HTL (High-Threshold Logic) is 24V compatible for PLC counting inputs. TTL needs 5V differential receiver. Z = index/reference pulse. ABZ + complements = 6 signal wires.

name: Resolver (Synchro) Cable
useCases: Servo motor position feedback (AC induction/PMSM), military servos, aerospace actuators, harsh environment motors
notes: Analog absolute position sensor — no electronics in the motor. Extremely rugged (no semiconductor junction). Resolver-to-digital converter (RDC) on drive side. Used where temp/vibration exceeds encoder limits.

name: Tamagawa Smartcoder / Nikon Encoder
useCases: Japanese servo motors (Yaskawa, Panasonic, Mitsubishi), industrial robots (FANUC), CNC machines
notes: Tamagawa AU6802/AU6805 protocol common on Yaskawa Sigma-series servos. Nikon encoders on some Panasonic MINAS series. Connector is OEM-specific — often 10-pin or 15-pin proprietary.

name: M23 Circular (Signal/Power)
useCases: Servo motor power connector, servo encoder feedback, CNC axis motors, industrial robot joints
notes: Larger than M12, used for combined servo motor power (3-phase + PE) or high-pin-count encoder feedback. Common on Siemens, SEW, Lenze, Beckhoff servo motors.

name: SpeedTec (Siemens proprietary)
useCases: Siemens SIMOTICS servo motors, SINAMICS drives, Siemens CNC (SINUMERIK) axes
notes: Siemens proprietary fast-locking servo connector. Quarter-turn instead of multi-turn thread saves installation time. Only compatible with Siemens ecosystem.

name: One Cable Technology (OCT/Hiperface DSL/SCS)
family: Various (Beckhoff OCT, Hiperface DSL, Rexroth SCS)
useCases: Modern servo systems — power + feedback on single cable, reducing cable cost and routing complexity
notes: Major trend in servo drives. Encoder data modulated on motor power cable. Beckhoff OCT, Rexroth SCS open, Hiperface DSL, FANUC αi serial — all different protocols. Not interchangeable across vendors.

name: USB Type-A (Standard)
useCases: Desktop PCs, laptops, chargers, flash drives, keyboards, mice, printers, all USB peripherals
notes: Most deployed USB connector in history. USB 3.x Type-A adds 5 pins behind the original 4. SuperSpeed 5/10Gbps. Being replaced by USB-C on new devices.

name: USB Type-B (Standard)
useCases: Printers, scanners, some audio interfaces, oscilloscopes (USBTMC), Arduino Uno/Mega
notes: Square-ish trapezoidal connector. USB 3.0 Type-B has a taller blue housing with extra pins on top. Still on many printers and lab instruments.

name: USB Mini-B (5-pin)
useCases: Older digital cameras, GPS navigators, Garmin devices, PS3 controllers, MP3 players
notes: Replaced by Micro-B. Still found on legacy Garmin GPS, old audio recorders. Mechanically weaker than Micro-B.

name: USB Micro-B (2.0)
useCases: Android phones (pre-2016), Raspberry Pi 2/3, Kindle, Bluetooth speakers, IoT devices, ESP32 dev boards
notes: Designed for 10,000 mating cycles (vs 1,500 for Mini-B). Still ubiquitous on ESP32 dev boards, budget IoT, and legacy Android devices.

name: USB Micro-B (3.0 SuperSpeed)
useCases: External HDDs, SSDs, USB 3.0 hubs, Samsung Galaxy Note 3/S5
notes: Wide dual-section connector. Fragile and unpopular — major reason USB-C was created. Only survived on external storage briefly.

name: USB Type-C
useCases: Modern phones (Android/iPhone 15+), laptops, Raspberry Pi 4/5, Jetson Orin, monitors, docks, VR headsets, drones, everything new
current: USB PD: up to 5A (240W at 48V EPR)
notes: Reversible. Supports USB 2.0 through USB4 (40/80Gbps), Thunderbolt 3/4/5, DisplayPort Alt Mode, HDMI Alt Mode. Not all USB-C cables/ports are equal — cable rating and Alt Mode support vary massively. EU mandates USB-C charging from 2024.

name: USB Type-C (USB4 / Thunderbolt 4/5)
useCases: Thunderbolt docks, eGPU enclosures, professional video I/O, daisy-chained monitors, high-speed storage arrays
notes: USB4 v2 supports 80Gbps symmetric or 120Gbps asymmetric. Thunderbolt 5 uses same USB-C connector with 80Gbps (120G asym via bandwidth boost). Active and optical cables extend reach.

name: Lightning
useCases: iPhone (5 through 14), iPad (older), AirPods, Magic Keyboard/Mouse/Trackpad
voltage: 5V (up to 9V with fast charge on some)
notes: Replaced 30-pin Dock connector in 2012. Being phased out — iPhone 15+ uses USB-C (EU mandate). MFi authentication chip required in cable/accessory.

name: Apple 30-pin Dock
useCases: iPhone 1–4S, iPad 1–3, iPod Classic/Nano/Touch, legacy Apple accessories
notes: Carried USB data, analog audio, composite/component video, serial, charging on one connector. Replaced by Lightning in 2012.

name: RJ45 (8P8C) — Ethernet
useCases: Ethernet 10/100/1000BASE-T/2.5G/5G/10GBASE-T, PoE, IP cameras, NAS, switches, routers, industrial PCs, data centers
notes: T568A and T568B wiring standards — don't mix. Cat 5e: 1Gbps, Cat 6: 1Gbps (10G at 55m), Cat 6A: 10Gbps at 100m, Cat 8: 25/40Gbps at 30m. PoE IEEE 802.3af/at/bt delivers up to 90W.

name: RJ45 Industrial (shielded, M12-style housing)
useCases: Industrial Ethernet in harsh environments, factory floor switches, machine vision cameras (non-M12)
locking: Latch + metal shield + sometimes screw housing
notes: IP20 to IP67 variants. Some use push-pull housings (Harting PushPull, TE ix Industrial) for vibration resistance. Shielded to prevent EMI in factory environments.

name: M12 D-coded (Ethernet, repeated from Phase 2)
useCases: See Phase 2. Included for cross-reference.
notes: 100Mbps industrial Ethernet standard. D-coded prevents mating with sensor A-coded M12.

name: M12 X-coded (Ethernet, repeated from Phase 2)
useCases: See Phase 2. Included for cross-reference.
notes: Gigabit/10G industrial Ethernet in M12 form factor.

name: ix Industrial (TE Connectivity)
useCases: Compact industrial Ethernet, robotics, servo drives with Ethernet, machine vision, Industry 4.0 edge devices
notes: 70% smaller than RJ45. Cat 6A capable (10Gbps). A-coded (Ethernet), B-coded (EtherCAT P). Growing adoption in compact industrial devices and collaborative robots.

name: SPE (Single Pair Ethernet) / T1 Industrial
useCases: IIoT sensor networks, building automation replacing BACnet, process automation replacing fieldbuses, automotive Ethernet
notes: Reduces Ethernet to a single twisted pair. Multiple connector form factors competing: IEC 63171-1 (LC-style), 63171-2 (industrial), 63171-5 (M8), 63171-6 (M12 hybrid). PoDL = Power over Data Line.

name: SFP/SFP+ Cage (LC fiber inside)
useCases: Network switches, routers, firewalls — 1G/10G/25G fiber or copper transceiver modules
maxLength: Depends on optic (up to 80km SM fiber)
notes: Hot-pluggable transceiver. SFP=1G, SFP+=10G, SFP28=25G. MSA (Multi-Source Agreement) ensures interop. LC duplex fiber interface on optical SFPs. RJ45 copper SFPs also exist.

name: QSFP/QSFP+/QSFP28/QSFP-DD Cage
useCases: Data center switches (40G/100G/200G/400G), HPC interconnect, InfiniBand, AI cluster networking
maxLength: Depends on optic (DAC: 5m, AOC: 100m, SM fiber: 10–40km)
notes: QSFP+=40G (4x10G), QSFP28=100G (4x25G), QSFP56=200G (4x50G), QSFP-DD=400G/800G. OSFP is competing form factor for 800G+. AI/ML clusters (Nvidia DGX) use QSFP heavily for InfiniBand/RoCE.

name: OSFP
useCases: 800G and 1.6T data center switches, next-gen AI cluster interconnect
notes: Larger than QSFP-DD, allows higher power dissipation for 800G/1.6T optics. Competing with QSFP-DD for next-gen data center standard.

name: CXP / CFP / CFP2 / CFP4
useCases: Older 40G/100G links, telecom long-haul, coherent optics
notes: CFP was first 100G MSA form factor — very large. CFP2/CFP4 shrunk it. Now mostly replaced by QSFP28/QSFP-DD except for coherent telecom optics where CFP2-DCO remains.

name: MTP/MPO Trunk Connector (fiber)
useCases: Data center structured cabling, 40G/100G/400G breakout, high-fiber-count trunks, cross-connects
notes: Multi-fiber push-on. MTP is US Conec's trademarked high-performance variant of MPO. 12-fiber is standard; 24/32 for 400G+ breakout. Gender: male (pins) / female (no pins). Polarity methods A/B/C defined by TIA-568.

name: LC Duplex (fiber, cross-ref Phase 4)
useCases: SFP transceivers, patch panels, FTTH ONT (some), 1G–25G per lane fiber links
notes: Small form factor. Dominant fiber connector for structured cabling and transceiver interfaces. Full details in Phase 4.

name: Ethernet Backplane (press-fit, high-speed)
family: Various (TE STRADA Whisper, Molex Mirror Mezz)
useCases: Switch fabric backplanes, chassis-based routers, blade server midplanes, telecom shelf backplanes
notes: Designed for 10G/25G/56G per lane signal integrity on backplane PCB traces. Controlled impedance, low crosstalk. TE STRADA Whisper supports 56Gbps PAM4.

name: FPC/FFC Connector (ZIF)
useCases: LCD/OLED display ribbons, camera modules (RPi CSI), touchscreen digitizers, laptop keyboard ribbons, drone FCs
locking: ZIF (Zero Insertion Force) flip-lock or slide-lock
notes: Zero Insertion Force — lift actuator, insert flex, close actuator. Pitch determines current/voltage rating. 0.5mm pitch is most common for displays. Top-contact and bottom-contact variants exist — verify orientation.

name: MIPI CSI-2 (on FPC)
useCases: Raspberry Pi camera (15-pin), Jetson camera (22-pin), drone cameras, embedded vision, ADAS camera modules
notes: MIPI CSI-2 is the protocol — physical connector varies by SBC/SoM vendor. RPi uses 15-pin 1mm FPC, Jetson uses 22-pin 0.5mm FPC. Not cross-compatible even if pin count matches — check pinout.

name: MIPI DSI (on FPC)
useCases: Raspberry Pi official display (15-pin), embedded LCD/OLED panels, smartphone displays
notes: MIPI DSI protocol on FPC connector. RPi DSI uses 15-pin 1mm FPC (different pinout from CSI despite identical connector). eDP is replacing DSI in some embedded displays.

name: HDMI Type A (Standard)
useCases: Monitors, TVs, projectors, gaming consoles, Raspberry Pi 4/5, media players, Jetson dev kits
notes: HDMI 2.0: 18Gbps (4K@60Hz). HDMI 2.1: 48Gbps (4K@120Hz, 8K@60Hz). No locking mechanism is a problem in industrial/AV — use panel-mount adapters or HDMI locking cable retention clips.

name: HDMI Type C (Mini)
useCases: DSLR cameras, camcorders, some tablets, drones (DJI), Raspberry Pi Zero (via adapter)
notes: Same signal capability as Type A in smaller form factor. Common on cameras for clean HDMI out. Fragile connector — avoid repeated mating.

name: HDMI Type D (Micro)
useCases: Smartphones (some), action cameras, ultra-compact devices, some Raspberry Pi accessories
notes: Smallest HDMI. Very fragile. Being replaced by USB-C Alt Mode for small devices.

name: DisplayPort (Standard / Full-size)
useCases: Desktop monitors, gaming monitors, graphics cards, professional displays, workstations, KVM switches
notes: DP 1.4: 32.4Gbps (4K@120Hz HDR, 8K@60Hz DSC). DP 2.1: up to 80Gbps (UHBR20). Supports daisy-chaining (MST). Latch clip is push-button release — better retention than HDMI. Free standard (no royalty, unlike HDMI).

name: Mini DisplayPort / Thunderbolt 1/2
useCases: Apple MacBooks (2008–2015), Surface Pro, some ultrabooks, Thunderbolt 1/2 devices
notes: Mini DisplayPort form factor reused for Thunderbolt 1 (10Gbps) and Thunderbolt 2 (20Gbps). TB3+ moved to USB-C. Still on some monitors as input.

name: DVI-D / DVI-I / DVI-A
useCases: Legacy monitors, projectors, KVM switches, older graphics cards, some medical displays
pins: DVI-D single: 19 / DVI-D dual: 25 / DVI-I: 29 / DVI-A: 17
notes: DVI-D = digital only, DVI-I = digital + analog, DVI-A = analog only. Single-link: 3.7Gbps (1920x1200@60Hz). Dual-link: 7.4Gbps (2560x1600@60Hz). Being removed from new GPUs.

name: PCIe x1/x4/x8/x16 Edge Connector
useCases: Graphics cards, NVMe SSDs (via adapter), network cards, FPGA accelerators, capture cards, AI accelerators (Nvidia A100/H100)
standard: PCI Express CEM spec, PCIe Gen 3/4/5/6
notes: PCIe Gen 3: 8GT/s (x16=32GB/s). Gen 4: 16GT/s (x16=64GB/s). Gen 5: 32GT/s (x16=128GB/s). Gen 6: 64GT/s (PAM4). CEM = Card Electromechanical. Edge fingers are gold-plated.

name: M.2 (NGFF) Connector
useCases: NVMe SSDs, WiFi/BT modules (Intel AX210), 5G/LTE modems, AI accelerators (Coral TPU), Jetson SoM carrier boards
notes: Keys determine interface: M-key = PCIe x4 (NVMe SSD), B-key = SATA/PCIe x2, E-key = PCIe x1 + USB (WiFi/BT), A-key = similar to E. Form factors: 2230/2242/2260/2280/22110 (width x length in mm). Jetson Orin uses M.2 for NVMe and WiFi.

name: U.2 (SFF-8639)
useCases: Enterprise NVMe SSDs (2.5"), server storage, data center drives
notes: Combines PCIe, SATA, and SAS on one connector. Hot-pluggable. Enterprise NVMe SSDs used U.2 before EDSFF. Cable connector is SFF-8643 (mini-SAS HD).

name: EDSFF E1.S / E1.L / E3.S
useCases: Next-gen data center NVMe SSDs, cloud server storage, hyperscale storage platforms
notes: Enterprise & Data Center SSD Form Factor. E1.S = short (replacing M.2 in servers), E1.L = long (ruler form), E3.S = 3" short. PCIe Gen 5 native. Replaces U.2 and M.2 in data center.

name: SATA Data (7-pin)
useCases: HDDs, SSDs, optical drives, SATA DOM (Disk on Module)
notes: SATA III max 6Gbps — bottleneck for modern SSDs. L-key prevents reverse insertion. SATA Express attempted PCIe on SATA connector — failed and abandoned.

name: SAS (Serial Attached SCSI) — SFF-8482 / SFF-8643
useCases: Enterprise HDDs/SSDs, server RAID arrays, SAN storage shelves, tape libraries
notes: Backward compatible with SATA drives (SAS controller accepts SATA drives, not vice versa). SFF-8643 mini-SAS HD is the modern internal/external cable connector. SFF-8644 for external.

name: OCuLink (SFF-9402)
useCases: Internal PCIe x4/x8 cable connections, eGPU docks (some), NVMe over cable, server GPU riser
notes: Cable version of PCIe for internal server connections. PCIe Gen 4 x4 = 64Gbps. Used in some compact eGPU docks. Being challenged by CXL for disaggregated compute.

name: CXL (Compute Express Link) — PCIe form factor
useCases: Memory expanders, accelerator attach, disaggregated memory pools, CXL switches, AI/ML infrastructure
pins: Same as PCIe CEM or EDSFF
notes: Uses PCIe physical layer but adds cache-coherent memory protocol. CXL 1.1 on Gen 5, CXL 2.0/3.0 on Gen 5/6. Same PCIe connector — differentiated by protocol. Critical for next-gen AI memory expansion.

name: NVLink / NVSwitch (Nvidia proprietary)
useCases: Nvidia DGX/HGX systems, multi-GPU AI training, H100/B200 GPU clusters
notes: NVLink 4 (H100): 900GB/s per GPU. NVLink 5 (B200): 1.8TB/s. NVSwitch allows full-bandwidth all-to-all GPU communication. Not a user-serviceable connector — part of baseboard/tray design.

name: MIL-DTL-38999 Series III
useCases: Military avionics, fighter aircraft (F-16/F-35), shipboard electronics, ground vehicles, satellite subsystems, ruggedized comms
notes: Tri-start bayonet allows quick connect with quarter-turn. EMI/RFI shielding. Environmental sealing IP67+. Scoop-proof design prevents pin damage. Series I = bayonet, Series II = threaded, Series III = tri-start bayonet, Series IV = push-pull. Multiple plating finishes (cadmium olive drab, nickel, electroless nickel).

name: MIL-DTL-38999 Series IV (Push-Pull)
useCases: Quick-disconnect military applications, soldier-worn electronics, manpack radios, field-deployed sensors
notes: Same insert arrangements as Series III but push-pull coupling for blind-mate or quick-disconnect applications.

name: MIL-DTL-5015 (MS/AN Circular)
useCases: Ground support equipment, older military vehicles, industrial motor connections, marine applications, power generation
notes: Oldest still-active MIL connector spec (dating to WWII). Large, robust, economical. Not EMI-shielded by default. Still widely used for ground equipment and industrial applications where environmental sealing is needed but EMI shielding isn't critical.

name: MIL-DTL-26482 Series I / II
useCases: Miniature military circular, missile electronics, UAV wiring, space-constrained avionics, helmet-mounted displays
notes: Miniature circular — smaller than 38999 for space-constrained military applications. Common on UAVs and missile systems.

name: MIL-DTL-83723 Series III
useCases: High-density avionics, mission computers, radar signal processing, EW systems
notes: Higher pin density than 38999 in similar shell sizes. Used where contact count is critical.

name: ARINC 600 (ATR Rack Connector)
useCases: Line Replaceable Units (LRUs) in ATR racks — flight computers, radios, GPS receivers, FMS, TCAS, transponders
notes: Standard avionics LRU connector. Blind-mate design — LRU slides into rack and connector mates automatically. Different insert sizes (1–7) for different LRU widths. DO-160 environmental qualification.

name: ARINC 404A / 404B (Legacy ATR)
useCases: Older ATR avionics racks, legacy LRUs still in service on older aircraft
notes: Predecessor to ARINC 600. Still found on legacy military aircraft and some older commercial avionics.

name: ARINC 801 (Fiber Optic Terminus)
useCases: Fiber optic connections in avionics, fly-by-light systems, high-bandwidth avionics data buses, AFDX networks
notes: Fiber optic termini that fit into standard MIL circular connector shells (38999, ARINC 600). Allows mixed copper/fiber in same connector.

name: ARINC 429 (on various connectors)
useCases: Primary avionics data bus — altitude, airspeed, heading, navigation data between LRUs on commercial aircraft
locking: Typically on ARINC 600 or D-sub within LRU
notes: Unidirectional, point-to-point or single-transmitter/multi-receiver. 12.5kbps (low speed) or 100kbps (high speed). 32-bit word format. Being supplemented by AFDX/ARINC 664 (Ethernet-based) on newer aircraft (A380, B787).

name: ARINC 664 / AFDX (on RJ45 or ARINC 600)
useCases: Avionics Full-Duplex Switched Ethernet — A380, A350, B787 avionics backbone, next-gen military avionics
notes: Deterministic Ethernet for safety-critical avionics. Virtual links with guaranteed bandwidth. Redundant network A/B. Physical layer is standard 100BASE-TX Ethernet.

name: MIL-STD-1553 (on MIL circular or D-sub)
useCases: Military avionics data bus, weapon systems, satellite buses, fighter/bomber avionics (F-16, B-2), UAVs, Indian LCA Tejas
pins: Twinax (1 shielded twisted pair per bus)
notes: 1Mbps half-duplex command/response bus. Dual-redundant (Bus A / Bus B). 31 remote terminals max per bus. Time-division multiplexing. Direct coupler (transformer) or stub coupling. Extremely mature and certified — used on nearly every military platform worldwide including Indian LCA Tejas and DRDO UAVs.

name: Fibre Channel (FC) — SFP or LC fiber
useCases: SAN storage networks, MIL-STD-1760 weapon store data, avionics high-speed bus, aircraft video distribution
maxLength: Depends on speed and medium (10km SM typical)
legacy: Declining in enterprise (NVMe-oF replacing), alive in mil
notes: FC-16G and FC-32G in enterprise SAN. Military uses FC for MIL-STD-1760 weapon datalink (smart weapons). Being replaced by NVMe-over-Fabrics in enterprise but entrenched in military avionics.

name: MIL-STD-1760 (Aircraft-Store Electrical Interconnect)
useCases: Interface between military aircraft and weapons/stores/pods — connects power, data, and safety to missiles, bombs, pods
notes: Defines the electrical interface between aircraft and everything hung under wings/fuselage. Carries MIL-STD-1553, Fibre Channel, discrete signals, power. Used on F-16, F-18, F-35, Eurofighter, Rafale, Indian Su-30MKI.

name: D-38999 Fiber Optic (TFOCA)
useCases: Tactical field fiber optic links, deployable comm systems, forward operating base networking
notes: Ruggedized fiber optic in 38999-style shells. TFOCA-II is the current standard. Used by all branches for tactical networking. IP68+ when mated.

name: Micro-D / Nano-D (MIL-DTL-83513 / MIL-DTL-32139)
useCases: Satellite PCBs, CubeSat wiring, missile guidance, implantable medical, UAV autopilots, space instruments
notes: Micro-D is half the size of standard D-sub. Nano-D is half of Micro-D. Critical for space applications where mass and volume are constrained. Omnetics and Glenair are major suppliers.

name: Hypertac / Hyperboloid Contact (in various shells)
useCases: Critical avionics connections requiring zero-defect contact, satellite bus connectors, nuclear instrumentation
notes: Hyperboloid contacts use twisted wire cage instead of leaf spring — provides multiple contact points, extremely low contact resistance, and long life (100,000+ cycles). Used inside 38999, ARINC 600, or proprietary shells for highest-reliability applications.

name: BNC (Bayonet Neill-Concelman)
useCases: Oscilloscope probes, signal generators, SDI video, CCTV analog, RF test equipment, 10BASE2 Ethernet (legacy), NIM/CAMAC nuclear instrumentation
maxLength: Depends on cable (RG-58: 100m at low freq)
notes: 50Ω (test/data) and 75Ω (video) variants exist — NOT interchangeable despite mating. 50Ω BNC usable to 4GHz, 75Ω to 2GHz. The universal lab/test connector.

name: TNC (Threaded Neill-Concelman)
useCases: Cellular base stations, military radios, GPS antennas, vibration-prone RF installations, avionics RF
maxLength: Same as BNC but better at higher freq
notes: Threaded version of BNC — better performance above 4GHz and in vibration environments. Used where BNC bayonet might loosen. Common on military and telecom RF.

name: N-Type (Neill)
useCases: Cell tower feedlines, broadcast transmitters, antenna systems, RF power amplifiers, radar, EMC test chambers
notes: 50Ω standard. Handles up to 11GHz. Higher power handling than BNC/TNC. Available in 75Ω variant for CATV/broadcast. Weatherproof when properly mated with tape/boot.

name: 7-16 DIN
useCases: Cell tower main feedlines, high-power broadcast, DAS (Distributed Antenna Systems), stadium/tunnel RF coverage
notes: Low PIM (Passive Intermodulation) design critical for multi-carrier cell sites. 7mm inner / 16mm outer conductor. Handles very high RF power (>100W continuous). Replacing N-type on modern cell towers.

name: 4.3-10 (Mini DIN)
useCases: Modern cell tower jumpers, small cells, indoor DAS, 5G antenna feeds, replacing 7-16 DIN in space-constrained installations
notes: 40% smaller than 7-16 DIN with comparable PIM performance. Multiple coupling mechanisms (screw, push-pull, snap) all intermate — major advantage for field installation flexibility.

name: SMA (SubMiniature version A)
useCases: RF modules, GPS antennas, WiFi pigtails, LoRa antennas, ISM band devices, VNA test ports, RF PCB transitions, satellite ground terminals
notes: 50Ω. Usable to 18GHz (standard) or 26.5GHz (precision). THE standard for RF board-to-cable and test equipment below 18GHz. Thread-on coupling — not quick-connect. LoRa, GPS, and WiFi antennas on Orin Nano / ESP32 typically use SMA or RP-SMA.

name: RP-SMA (Reverse Polarity SMA)
useCases: WiFi routers, WiFi antennas, LoRa gateway antennas, FPV drone video TX, Bluetooth antennas, Zigbee
notes: Same shell as SMA but pin/socket reversed — male RP-SMA has socket (no center pin), female has pin. Created to prevent consumers from attaching high-gain antennas to WiFi gear (FCC compliance). RP-SMA and SMA do NOT mate.

name: SMB (SubMiniature version B)
useCases: Telecom equipment, automotive RF (being replaced by FAKRA), GPS timing, test jigs, PCB-mount RF
notes: Quick-connect snap-on — faster than SMA threaded. 50Ω or 75Ω. Usable to 4GHz. Automotive applications largely replaced by FAKRA. Still used in telecom timing distribution.

name: SMC (SubMiniature version C)
useCases: Instrumentation, military RF, telecom, some medical RF equipment
notes: Threaded version of SMB — less common. Same frequency range as SMB but more secure connection.

name: MCX
useCases: GPS modules (u-blox), cellular modems, IoT boards, PCB-mount RF, portable instruments
notes: 30% smaller than SMB. Snap-on for quick mating. 50Ω. Usable to 6GHz. Common on u-blox GPS modules and cellular modem boards.

name: MMCX
useCases: WiFi/BT modules, GPS modules (tiny boards), laptop WiFi cards, in-ear monitor wireless modules
notes: Micro-miniature. 45% smaller than MCX. 360° rotation after mating — useful for routing flexibility. 50Ω. Usable to 6GHz. Common on Intel WiFi M.2 cards.

name: U.FL / IPEX / MHF (Hirose)
useCases: WiFi/BT antennas on PCBs (ESP32, Raspberry Pi, Jetson), cellular module antenna, GPS module antenna, IoT devices
notes: Smallest common RF connector. 1.4mm mating height. 50Ω. Usable to 6GHz. Very fragile — rated for only ~30 mating cycles. U.FL (Hirose), MHF/I-PEX (Dai-ichi Seiko), and AMC are functionally identical but not officially cross-brand. Used on Xiao ESP32-S3 and most WiFi-enabled SBCs.

name: MHF4 / MHF4L (I-PEX)
useCases: Ultra-thin laptops, smartphones, M.2 WiFi 6E/7 cards, 5G module antennas
notes: Even smaller than U.FL/MHF. 0.8mm height. Used on latest Intel/Qualcomm WiFi 6E/7 M.2 modules. NOT compatible with U.FL/MHF — different mating interface.

name: SSMA / SSMC / SMP / SMPM
useCases: Millimeter-wave test fixtures, 5G mmWave modules, phased array antennas, satellite payloads, radar T/R modules
notes: SSMA: 40GHz. SMP/SMPM: push-on blind-mate to 40GHz+. Used in 5G mmWave and phased array where hundreds of RF connections needed. SMPM bullet adapters for board-to-board RF.

name: 2.92mm (K-Connector, Wiltron)
useCases: VNA test ports, microwave test fixtures, satellite ground station IF, radar IF distribution
notes: DC to 40GHz. Mates with SMA and 3.5mm connectors (same thread). Common as primary connector on 40GHz-class VNAs (Keysight PNA/ENA).

name: 2.4mm
useCases: 50GHz VNA test ports, millimeter-wave component testing, 5G FR2 test, satellite Ka-band
notes: DC to 50GHz. Does NOT mate with SMA (different thread). Mates with 1.85mm connector. Used on high-end VNAs.

name: 1.85mm (V-Connector)
useCases: 67GHz VNA test, W-band component test, advanced radar, satellite payload testing
notes: DC to 67GHz. Mates with 2.4mm connector. Fragile and expensive.

name: 1.0mm
useCases: 110GHz VNA test, terahertz research, next-gen communication research, W/D-band testing
notes: DC to 110GHz. Highest frequency standard coaxial connector. Extremely fragile. Used on Keysight/R&S/Anritsu top-tier VNAs.

name: F-Type (75Ω)
useCases: Cable TV (CATV), satellite TV (DTH), terrestrial antenna (DVB-T), cable modems (DOCSIS), CCTV distribution
pins: 1 (coaxial, center conductor is cable center conductor)
notes: 75Ω only. Unique — center pin of connector IS the cable's center conductor (no separate contact). Cheap but impedance-sensitive to installation quality. Crimp, compression, or push-on termination.

name: PAL/Belling-Lee (IEC 61169-2)
useCases: TV antenna connection (European/Indian/Australian standard), FM antenna, DTH set-top box RF loop
notes: Standard TV antenna connector in India (Doordarshan/DTH RF output), Europe, Australia. Male = plug with center pin, Female = jack (socket). Being phased out as TVs move to HDMI/streaming.

name: QMA
useCases: Cell site jumpers, DAS, military tactical radios (quick-disconnect), test equipment
notes: Same electrical performance as SMA but with snap-lock instead of threaded — 75% faster mating. Gaining traction in telecom and tactical military applications.

name: LC (Lucent Connector)
useCases: SFP/SFP+ transceivers, data center patch panels, FTTH ONT (some), telecom MDF, enterprise LAN fiber
notes: Dominant fiber connector for structured cabling and transceivers. Half the size of SC. Duplex clip joins two simplex LCs. UPC (blue) and APC (green angled) polish variants.

name: SC (Subscriber Connector / Square Connector)
useCases: FTTH ONT (GPON/EPON), older telecom patch panels, CATV fiber nodes, some BSNL/Jio FTTH installations in India
notes: Larger than LC. Push-pull snap-in mechanism. APC variant (angled, green) standard for FTTH/CATV to minimize back-reflection. Very common on GPON ONTs in India (BSNL FTTH, Jio Fiber).

name: ST (Straight Tip)
useCases: Legacy LAN fiber, older campus fiber backbone, military fiber (some), multimode building cabling
notes: Bayonet-coupled — twist to lock. Was dominant LAN fiber connector before LC/SC. Still found in older buildings and some military installations. No duplex version.

name: FC (Ferrule Connector)
useCases: Telecom test equipment, OTDR, fiber laser, precision measurement, single-mode instrumentation, DWDM systems
notes: Screw-on for vibration resistance and repeatability. APC variant critical for analog CATV and DWDM. Being replaced by LC-APC in most new installations. Still standard on some OTDR and test equipment.

name: MTP/MPO (cross-ref from Phase 3)
useCases: See Phase 3. Data center 40G/100G/400G breakout trunks, high-fiber-count cross-connects.
notes: Multi-fiber push-on. 12F standard; 24/32F for 400G+. Polarity A/B/C methods. MTP (US Conec) is high-performance MPO.

name: E2000 (LSH)
useCases: European telecom, Deutsche Telekom standard, DWDM systems, high-reliability telecom installations
notes: Integrated spring-loaded protective cap covers ferrule when unmated — prevents contamination. APC variant standard for DWDM. Dominant in European telecom but rare elsewhere.

name: MU (Miniature Unit)
useCases: Japanese telecom (NTT), high-density fiber patch panels, older Japanese FTTH
notes: Same 1.25mm ferrule as LC but different latch mechanism. NTT-developed. Functionally similar to LC but not interchangeable.

name: FDDI/MIC Connector
useCases: FDDI ring networks (100Mbps fiber, 1990s campus backbone)
notes: FDDI (Fiber Distributed Data Interface) was pre-Ethernet high-speed LAN. Dual counter-rotating ring. Completely replaced by Ethernet.

name: SMA 905/906 (Fiber)
useCases: Fiber-coupled laser diodes, spectrometers, medical fiber probes, industrial fiber sensors, legacy multimode
locking: Threaded coupling (same as RF SMA thread)
legacy: Declining in telecom, alive in specialty
notes: Same thread as RF SMA but carries fiber — do not confuse with RF SMA. 905 = flat ferrule, 906 = stepped ferrule. Still used for specialty fiber (medical probes, spectroscopy, power delivery fiber).

name: Expanded Beam Connector (EB)
useCases: Military tactical fiber, shipboard fiber, outdoor industrial fiber, mining, subsea (some)
notes: Uses lenses to expand beam at connector face — dramatically reduces sensitivity to dirt, scratches, and misalignment. Critical for field-deployed military fiber. Higher insertion loss than physical-contact (PC) connectors.

name: XLR-3 (3-pin)
useCases: Professional microphones, mixing consoles, PA systems, studio monitors, DMX512 lighting control (sometimes)
notes: THE professional audio connector. Balanced connection rejects noise over long runs. Male = output (mic), Female = input (mixer). AES/EBU digital audio also uses XLR-3 (110Ω). Neutrik is dominant manufacturer.

name: XLR-4 (4-pin)
useCases: Professional camera DC power (12V), intercom headsets (Clear-Com, RTS), follow-focus power, cinema lighting power
notes: 4-pin XLR used for DC power in cinema/broadcast. Arri, RED, Canon cinema cameras use 4-pin XLR for 12V power input. Also standard for professional intercom headsets.

name: XLR-5 (5-pin)
useCases: DMX512 lighting control (proper connector per standard), dual-channel intercom, some stereo microphones
notes: DMX512 standard specifies 5-pin XLR (not 3-pin, despite 3-pin being commonly used). Pin 4/5 reserved for secondary data pair. Neutrik and Switchcraft are main suppliers.

name: XLR-7 (7-pin)
useCases: Some tube microphone power supplies (high voltage + audio), specialized cinema applications
notes: Niche usage — primarily tube condenser microphone external PSUs that carry high voltage for tube bias plus balanced audio return.

name: Neutrik Speakon (NL2/NL4/NL8)
useCases: PA system speaker cables, stage monitors, subwoofers, amplifier-to-speaker connections, touring sound
notes: Replaced 1/4" TS for speaker connections in professional audio — touch-safe, locking, high current. NL4 carries bi-amp (2 channels). NL8 for tri-amp or quad-amp. Industry standard for live sound.

name: Neutrik powerCON (NAC3FCA/FCB)
useCases: Stage lighting power, powered speakers, amplifier racks, DJ equipment, LED wall panels
notes: Blue = power IN, Gray = power OUT (daisy-chain). NOT hot-pluggable (unlike powerCON TRUE1). Replaced IEC and stage pin in many entertainment applications. Based on Neutrik XLR-style shell.

name: Neutrik powerCON TRUE1
useCases: Hot-pluggable stage power, LED wall power, touring rigs requiring live-connect
notes: TRUE1 variant is rated for mating/unmating under load — critical distinction from standard powerCON.

name: 1/4" TRS (6.35mm Stereo/Balanced)
useCases: Headphones (studio), balanced line-level (mixers, outboard gear), insert points, guitar effects send/return
notes: Tip=Left/Hot+, Ring=Right/Cold-, Sleeve=Ground. TRS can carry balanced mono OR unbalanced stereo — context-dependent. Locking variants exist (Neutrik locking jack).

name: 1/4" TS (6.35mm Mono)
useCases: Electric guitar/bass cables, keyboard outputs, amplifier inputs, effects pedal patch cables
notes: High-impedance instrument level. Susceptible to noise over long runs. Always use shielded instrument cable, not speaker cable.

name: 3.5mm TRS/TRRS (1/8" mini-jack)
useCases: Headphones (consumer), earphones, laptop audio, smartphone headset (TRRS), aux input, MIDI TRS (see Phase 2)
notes: TRRS adds microphone contact. Two TRRS standards: CTIA (Tip=L, Ring1=R, Ring2=GND, Sleeve=Mic) used by Apple/Samsung/most, OMTP (Ring2=Mic, Sleeve=GND) used by older Nokia/Sony. Incompatible pinouts cause mic/ground swap.

name: 2.5mm TRS/TRRS
useCases: Two-way radio headsets (Motorola, Kenwood), older cell phone headsets, some camcorder mics
notes: Smaller than 3.5mm. Still standard on Motorola/Kenwood/Baofeng two-way radio headsets (often combined with 3.5mm in dual-plug headset). Being replaced by Bluetooth/USB-C.

name: RCA (Phono Connector)
useCases: Home audio (stereo L/R), subwoofer output (LFE), composite video (yellow), component video (YPbPr), S/PDIF coaxial digital audio
legacy: Declining for video, alive for audio
notes: Color-coded: White/Red = stereo audio L/R, Yellow = composite video, Red/Green/Blue = component video (YPbPr), Orange = S/PDIF digital. 75Ω for video/S/PDIF. Ubiquitous in home audio.

name: TOSLINK (Optical S/PDIF)
useCases: Soundbar connection, TV optical audio out, AV receivers, CD/DVD players, digital audio interfaces
notes: Toshiba-developed. Carries PCM stereo or compressed 5.1 (Dolby Digital/DTS). Cannot carry lossless surround (Atmos) — use HDMI eARC instead. Mini-TOSLINK uses 3.5mm form factor (found on older MacBooks).

name: BNC (SDI Video)
useCases: Broadcast cameras, video routers, production switchers, broadcast monitors, OB vans, cinema DIT stations
notes: SDI = Serial Digital Interface. Uses 75Ω BNC (NOT 50Ω test BNC). Uncompressed video. SD-SDI: 270Mbps, HD-SDI: 1.485Gbps, 3G-SDI: 2.97Gbps, 12G-SDI: 11.88Gbps (4K). Broadcast standard globally.

name: Lemo (0B/1B/2B Push-Pull)
useCases: Cinema cameras (RED DSMC2/V-Raptor), broadcast ENG, precision instruments, medical devices, military signaling, UAV payloads
notes: Swiss precision connector. Self-latching push-pull is the defining feature. Used in any application demanding quick, secure, repeated connection in premium/high-reliability context. Expensive but excellent.

name: Fischer Core Series (Push-Pull)
useCases: Military manpack radios, broadcast cameras, industrial sensors, UAV data links, soldier-worn systems
notes: Direct competitor to LEMO. IP68 sealed variants available. Used extensively in defense (soldier-worn electronics, tactical comms). Fischer Freedom series is breakaway contact.

name: ODU AMC/MINI-SNAP Push-Pull
useCases: Medical instruments, endoscopes, test equipment, broadcast, industrial robots, military
notes: German competitor to LEMO/Fischer. AMC = Advanced Military Connector. MINI-SNAP for commercial. Break-away variant for safety-critical applications (surgical instruments).

name: SocaPex (19-pin)
useCases: Stage lighting multi-cable, theater dimmer-to-lighting bar, touring rig power distribution
notes: Carries 6 independent 20A circuits in one connector — replaces 6 individual powerCON or stage pin runs. Standard for theater/concert stage lighting infrastructure.

name: EtherCON (Neutrik)
useCases: Stage networking, digital audio snakes (Dante/AES67), broadcast OB trucks, LED wall data
locking: XLR-style latch in D-series panel cutout
notes: Standard RJ45 cable inside ruggedized XLR-style housing. Protects fragile RJ45 tab in touring environments. Panel-mount uses standard D-series XLR cutout.

name: opticalCON (Neutrik)
useCases: Broadcast fiber runs, OB truck fiber breakout, stadium fiber distribution, touring stage fiber
notes: Ruggedized fiber in XLR-style housing. LC duplex inside. Dust caps and rugged shell protect fiber in touring/broadcast field use.

name: Standard Thermocouple Connector (flat 2-pin)
useCases: Thermocouple extension to instruments, furnace monitoring, industrial ovens, kilns, HVAC, boilers
notes: Color-coded by TC type: Yellow=K, Red/Blue=T, Red/White=J, Red/Purple=E (ANSI). IEC uses different colors. Contacts must be same alloy as TC wire — standard copper contacts introduce measurement error. Male=flat prongs, Female=rounded sockets.

name: Miniature Thermocouple Connector
useCases: Compact TC probes, handheld thermometers, portable data loggers, surface probes
notes: Same color-coding and alloy rules as standard TC connectors. Smaller footprint for handheld instruments. OMEGA brand connectors are de facto standard.

name: RTD 3-wire/4-wire Terminal
useCases: PT100/PT1000 RTD sensors, pharmaceutical process, food processing, HVAC precision, laboratory
notes: RTD sensors use generic terminals — no dedicated connector. 3-wire compensates for lead resistance partially. 4-wire eliminates lead resistance error completely. PT100 = 100Ω at 0°C. Often terminated on M12 A-coded in industrial settings.

name: Load Cell Connector (4-wire/6-wire)
useCases: Weighing scales, batching systems, crane load limiters, force measurement, tensile testers, materials testing
locking: Depends on connector (screw TB, D-sub, military circular)
notes: No universal connector standard — load cells use whatever connector the manufacturer specifies. Typical: shielded 4-core cable with shield as 5th conductor. High-end uses 6-wire (adds sense lines to compensate cable resistance). Binder circular or D-sub common on precision instruments.

name: Accelerometer / Vibration Sensor (BNC or 10-32 Microdot)
